content.problemStatement:
A balanced parenthesis string is defined as follows:


The empty string is balanced.
If `P` is balanced, `(P)` is also balanced.
If `P` and `Q` are balanced, `PQ` is also balanced.
`"()"`, `"(())()"` are examples of balanced parenthesis strings while `"(()"`, `"())"` are not balanced.

A substring of a string is a contiguous subsection of the given string.

Given nnn and kkk, output a balanced parenthesis string of length nnn having exactly kkk substrings which are also balanced. If no such string is possible, output `"impossible"` without quotes. If there are multiple answers, print any.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, which has two integers, nnn and kkk, separated by a space.


Output Format
For each test case, output any balanced parenthesis string which satisfies the condition or `"impossible"` if no such string exists.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of test cases T. Each test case consists of a single line with two integers n and k, separated by a space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a balanced parenthesis string of length n having exactly k substrings which are also balanced. If no such string is possible, output 'impossible'.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Case 1: The entire string is also considered as its own substring, so there is exactly 1 balanced substring: () itself. Case 2: No balanced string with length 5 exists. Case 3: There are exactly 4 substrings that are balanced (indices are 0-based): Substring 0 to 1: (), Substring 3 to 4: (), Substring 2 to 5: (()), Substring 0 to 5: ()(())

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a greedy approach. We start by constructing a string of n parentheses. We then try to add as many balanced substrings as possible to the string while keeping the total number of substrings less than or equal to k. We can do this by adding pairs of parentheses to the string, which will increase the number of balanced substrings by the number of pairs added.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n) — the time complexity is linear with respect to the number of test cases and the length of the string.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) — the space complexity is linear with respect to the length of the string.
